macro_segments.title: Introduction to Masechet Temurah: The Prohibition and Its Halachic Effects

micro_segments.title: Overview of Masechet Temurah and the phenomenon of prohibited acts with halachic impact
The paradox: Lo yachalifenu velo yamir oto (prohibition) yet Ve'im hamir yamir (halachic effect)
The linguistic issue: What does Hakol memirin mean? Everybody can cause kedusha to transfer
Clarification via Rav Yehuda: The word memirin means the act is effective, not that it is permitted

macro_segments.title: The Core Question: Is Temurah's Effect a Consequence of Sin or Metaphysical Power?

micro_segments.title: The framing of the question: Is temurah effect tied to prohibition/sin or to kedusha power itself?

macro_segments.title: The Definition of Owner: Does an Heir Qualify to Perform Temurah and Semichah?

micro_segments.title: The question Hakol le'atuyei mai: What does everybody include? Introduction of the heir case
Rabbi Meir: Heir is like a new owner; he does semichah and can do temurah
Rabbi Yehuda: Heir is not the owner; learns from end of kedusha (semichah) to beginning (temurah)
Three instances of korbano in the Torah: exclusion of non-Jews, friends, and fathers' sacrifices
Rabbi Meir's interpretation: Korbano means each owner in cases of partnership

macro_segments.title: Women and Temurah: Inclusion through Redundant Lingual Forms

micro_segments.title: The question of how women are included given that the parsha is written in male form
The double form Hamir yamir as inclusive of both heirs and women; both views agree women are included
The philosophical question: Why would we have excluded women from a negative prohibition at all?

macro_segments.title: The Definition of Owner: Does an Heir Qualify to Perform Temurah and Semichah? (continued)

micro_segments.title: The difference between communal sacrifices and partnership sacrifices regarding temurah and semichah

macro_segments.title: Women and Temurah: Inclusion through Redundant Lingual Forms (continued)

micro_segments.title: The uniqueness of temurah: A prohibition where only speech is required to transgress
Rashi's alternative girsa: Temurah as a chiddush (novelty) makes it potentially exceptional

macro_segments.title: The Core Question: Is Temurah's Effect a Consequence of Sin or Metaphysical Power? (continued)

micro_segments.title: Case of the minor: Can a katan who is not yet subject to punishment still perform effective temurah?
If temurah is punishment, a minor approaching bar/bat mitzvah would not be punishable yet
If temurah is metaphysical, a minor with kedusha powers could still perform effective temurah

macro_segments.title: The Non-Jew Case: Testing the Relationship Between Sin and Temurah Effect

micro_segments.title: The parallel case of a non-Jew: He has kedusha power but will never be subject to punishment

macro_segments.title: The Core Question: Is Temurah's Effect a Consequence of Sin or Metaphysical Power? (continued) (continued)

micro_segments.title: Two frameworks: consequence of sin versus membership in the parsha (category)

macro_segments.title: The Non-Jew Case: Testing the Relationship Between Sin and Temurah Effect (continued)

micro_segments.title: Baraita response: Non-Jews' sacrifices have weaker kedusha; no temurah can be performed on their korban
Distinctions in non-Jewish sacrifices: No nehonin, no moalin, no piggul/notar/tumah prohibitions
Rashi's reading of ve'ein mevi'in nesachim: Non-Jews cannot bring drink offerings separately
Rabbi Shimon vs Rabbi Yosi: Debate on the level of kedusha in non-Jews' sacrifices at the altar
The unresolved case: Non-Jew sanctifies for an Israelite—would temurah work with full kedusha?

topical_tags.term: Lo yachalifenu velo yamir oto (prohibition against temurah)
Ve'im hamir yamir behema behema (halachic effect despite prohibition)
Hakol matpisin b'temurah (everyone can cause kedusha to transfer)
lashon zachar (male form of language in Torah)
Rabbi Meir vs Rabbi Yehuda (heir ownership dispute)
sin and transgression as basis for halachic effect
Rabbi Shimon vs Rabbi Yosi (kedusha level debate)
kadshei mizbe'ach (sacrifices dedicated to the altar)
mitkaper (one for whom atonement is made)
